🇦🇪🇮🇷 The UAE just released its war numbers and one stands out

Since the war started: 357 ballistic missiles, 15 cruise missiles, and 1,815 drones fired at the UAE alone. 

Three military personnel and six civilians killed, 166 injured.

But today's count was 9 drones and zero ballistic missiles. That's a potential shift. 

Iran may be running low on the short-range ballistic missiles it has been using to target the Emirates. 

Drones are cheap and easy to produce. 

Ballistic missiles aren't, and every factory that built them has been bombed for four straight weeks.

This matters beyond the Gulf. 

Those same ballistic missiles, fitted with cluster warheads that split into dozens of bomblets at 23,000 feet, have been one of Iran's most devastating tools against Israel.

A single warhead shatters into so many pieces that even Iron Dome and David's Sling can't track them all, which is how Iran caused over 100 casualties in Arad and kept hitting Tel Aviv night after night. 

If the missile stockpile is genuinely depleting, both the Gulf and Israel could see the pressure ease significantly.

Zero ballistic missiles today could be a blip. 

Or it could be the first sign that four weeks of factory strikes are finally showing results.

Source: @Osint613 AI Telly

🇮🇷🇮🇱 Iran sent another cluster munition into Kafr Qasim in central Israel this morning, wounding two people, a 55-year-old man and woman, when a bomblet hit a building.

https://t.co/n7VU18rB9j

https://t.co/12e3HCaq2q

🚨 🇮🇷 WAR AS SANCTIONS RELIEF

Expert writer Trita Parsi points out a paradox: the war has actually provided Iran with de facto sanctions relief.

Iran is exporting more oil at nearly triple the pre-war price due to the conflict, according to Parsi.

Trita argues that because of this, Iran will view significant, formal sanctions relief as a non-negotiable term for peace.

@tparsi

🇺🇸🇮🇷 Many of the 13 U.S. military bases across the region are "all but uninhabitable." 

Kuwait's bases took the worst damage, being closest to Iran. 

Six Americans were killed when a strike destroyed a tactical operations center at Port Shuaiba. 

Ali Al Salem took hits on aircraft structures. 

Camp Buehring lost maintenance and fuel facilities.

In Qatar, CENTCOM's own regional air headquarters had its early-warning radar damaged. 

In Bahrain, the 5th Fleet HQ took a drone to its comms equipment. 

In Saudi Arabia, Prince Sultan Air Base lost several refueling tankers and communications gear.

The Pentagon said Iran's military was "decimated." Nobody mentioned that America's regional basing infrastructure was getting gutted in return. 

Four weeks of nightly attacks with cheap drones and missiles rendered some of the most important U.S. military installations in the world effectively unusable.

This is the cost nobody in Washington wants to talk about while they celebrate strike counts.

Source: New York Times

🇺🇸🇮🇱 U.S. and Israeli strikes are intensifying fast and spreading deeper into Iran.

They’ve now hit Shiraz, Lamerd Airport, Isfahan, Karaj, Bandar Abbas, Mashhad, and Taybad.
https://t.co/Q5FbQqveZd

🚨🇮🇷  THE MYSTERY OF MOJTABA

Trita Parsi, Executive VP of the Quincy Institute, weighs in on reports that Mojtaba Khamenei is now calling the shots.

While a close ally was appointed to the Security Council, Parsi notes there is no confirmation of Mojtaba's current health.

Trita explains that while speculation is high, the Iranian system remains a "black box" regarding its top leadership.

@tparsi

China's industrial sector is under heavy pressure:

23.8% of Chinese industrial firms were unprofitable in 2025, the highest since 2000 and doubling since 2017.

This marks the 4th consecutive annual increase, their worst streak on record.

Profit margins for private industrial firms are down to just 4.5%, the lowest since at least 2014 and down for 4 straight years.

This comes as China continues to battle the longest deflation streak on record, weak domestic demand, and now uncertainty about energy costs due to the Iran War.

China's industrial recovery remains nowhere in sight.

BREAKING: Foreign holdings of US Treasuries surged +$34.8 billion in January, to $9.3 trillion, the 2nd-highest on record.

Japan, the largest foreign-owner, purchased +$39.8 billion, bringing the total to $1.2 trillion, the highest since July 2022.

The UK, the 2nd-largest holder, posted a +$29.3 billion increase, to $895.3 billion, the 3rd-highest on record.

China's stockpile, the 3rd-largest holder, increased +$10.9 billion, to $694.4 billion, the highest since September.

Meanwhile, European Union holdings rose +$8.0 billion, to $2.1 trillion.

Foreigners now own more US debt than ever.

Fertilizer prices are skyrocketing:

New Orleans granular urea prices, the most widely used nitrogen fertilizer, are up +89% since December, to $660 per short ton, the highest since September 2022.

By comparison, the 2022 peak during the Russia-Ukraine war was $910 per short ton.

This comes as the Strait of Hormuz shutdown has effectively halted fertilizer shipments from the Middle East.

Furthermore, top exporters China and Russia are also curbing crop nutrient sales, tightening supply further at the worst possible time, right before the spring planting season.

Higher fertilizer costs directly translate to higher food prices, threatening to reignite agricultural inflation as seen in 2022.

Food is set to get even more expensive.

This is good to see

$ORBS

@RyanDetrick put together fi…

Intraday market volatility has rarely been this elevated:

Front-month $VIX futures intra-day volatility is up to 388.2, the highest reading in at least 6 months.

This index tracks $VIX futures price swings within a single trading day, measured in 15-minute intervals.

The current reading is ~4 TIMES higher than the average level, usually associated with full-blown market panic.

Meanwhile, S&P 500 implied volatility, showing how much the index is expected to move in a day, is up to ~1.77%, the highest since April 2025.

Despite this, the number of sessions with moves greater than 1.75% over the last 3 months is just 2.

Put simply, futures and options markets are pricing in far more volatility than the S&P 500 is actually realizing.

Uncertainty is at unprecedented levels.

BREAKING: Russia is close to completing a phased shipment of drones, medicine, and food to Iran, per FT.

Details include:

1. Russia and Iran reportedly began secretly discussing delivering drones days after Israel and the US attacked Tehran

2. Russia has been providing Iran with "crucial support" including satellite imagery, targeting data, and intelligence

3. Advanced Russian weaponry could improve the effectiveness of Iranian drone strikes

4. Russia has declined Iranian requests for the S-400, one of Moscow’s most advanced air defense systems

Russia's support for Iran remains strong.

The market is experiencing historically high intraday volatility:

The number of intraday reversals in the S&P 500 over the last 3 months is up to 28, the highest since 2015.

This means that in nearly half of all trading sessions over the last 3 months, the market opened either higher or lower than the previous day and erased that opening move.

This is also the 2nd-highest reading since the 2008 Financial Crisis, which peaked at 35 such days.

Headline-induced volatility is extremely high.

BREAKING: Iran says it could shut the vital Bab el-Mandeb Strait if attacks are carried out on its territory or islands.

Details include:

1. The Bab al-Mandeb Strait is a narrow passage linking the Red Sea to the Gulf of Aden and the Arabian Sea

2. ~12% of global seaborne oil passes through Bab al-Mandab

3. The Bab al-Mandeb Strait is the world’s 4th-largest shipping chokepoint

4. If both Hormuz and Mandeb are closed, total offline capacity could near 25 million barrels per day, or ~25% of global supply

We now await the US' response to Iran's "five conditions" for a ceasefire.

BREAKING: Iran says no negotiations will take place until their "five conditions" are met.

Iran also says it will end the war at a time of its own choosing and only if the conditions it has set are fulfilled.

As we noted last year around this time, there is always a reason stocks lose double digits during a calendar year.

Only 12 times since 1928 has the S&P 500 finished down double digits and each one had a reason. https://t.co/8bqfgijTgs

Small sample size, yes.

Still, last year saw the S&P 500 down more than 15% YTD at some point and finished the year up more than double digits. We called this a slingshot year.

The other three times that happened (1982, 2009, and 2020) saw the following year up at least double digits each time and up 19.0% on average.
